column2: This was an area of steep bedrock, outcrops and gullies. Kelp park species included Polyneura gmelinii, Sycpha coronata and Gymnangium montagui.

column2: At this site, at the Southern tip of the Island, the shore was composed of broken, rocky slopes with a south-west aspect. These rocks were mainly Balanus covered, with Chthamalus belt at the top of thje shore. Some Pelvetia was present. At one point a limestone band 2m wide extended right up the shore; it's surface was pitted with depressions occupied by Patella and was covered by encrusting coralline algae and Corallina to the top of the eulittoral. Some Chondrus was also present amongst high-shore Pelvetia, an indication of the great exposure to waves and the water retaining property of the eroded limestone surface. The most exposed parts of this shore were not visited.

column2: Backed by low cliffs of boulder clay, this medim-sloping rock flattened out at the lower levels; its aspect was SW. Pelvetia was present above; encrusting corallines covered most of the lower shore. There were many shallow coralline pools. A Barnacle/Patella cover was dominant, interspersed with occasional fucoids.
column3: Backed by low cliffs of boulder clay, this medium-sloping rock flattened out at the lower levels; its aspect was SW. column2: A series of quite steep rises and falls resulted in a narrow algal zone - the Laminaria forest extended only a few metres from the shore. In the deeper water between about 12.5m and 175m there was a carpet of red algae but at a depth of about 20m the algae ceased to grow. Below this there was a slope of large boulders with pockets of stony gravel. Alcyonium digitatum and Tubularia indivisa were common on the boulders. The larger boulders gave way to smaller ones on the bottom at a depth of about 30m.
column3: A series of quite steep rises and falls resulted in a narrow algal zone - the Laminaria forest extended only a few metres from the shore. The larger boulders gave way to smaller ones on the bottom at a depth of about 30m.

column2: The Laminaria forest extended out from the shore for a distance of approximately 50m where it ended at a depth of about 7my on a steep bedrock and large boulder slope.
column3: The Laminaria forest extended out from the shore for a distance of approximately 50m where it ended at a depth of about 7m on a steep bedrock and large boulder slope.

column2: A flat rocky area facing south-easterly with boulders and some silty mud and sand, mainly covered by Ascophyllum, some Fucus vesiculosus and F. serratus. Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthalia at lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, wiht other annelids.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay. Pelvetia, Ascophyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whilst lower down there were many overhangs and crevices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered with sand and silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders,h together with Platynereis coccinea.

The major cover was Baalnus, Chthamalus and Patella with occasional fucoids.

column2: The whole surface must be highly mobile and is thrown up into a series of large sand waves over 1m in height and running at right angles to the shore. The mobility and lack of hard substrates restricted the fauna to a few species of fish such as Plaice and Brill. Some hermit crabs, Pagurus bernhardus were also present. The landward edge of the bank slopes downwards at an angle of about 30 degrees to a depth of about 25m where boulders and rock outcrops begin to occur. Some tube sof the polychaete Lanice conchilega were present on the slope. Prominent species on the boulders were Nemertesia ramosa, Bugula turbinata, Tealia felina and Flustra foliacea. The latter two species have been recorded in areas of the Menai Strait which are similarly subject to gravel abrasion.

column2: This was a gently sloping bedrock shelf, wit hLaminaria hyperborea and Dictyota dichotoma. Just to the south of this site is a reef which extends seawards and protects, to some extent, the shallow plateau from the strong currents. Consequently, more silt deposition was recorded in the Laminaria forest than elsewhere on the profile.

column2: This was the only site which consisted mostly of soft bottom material occasionally interrupted by a small rock outcrop. Three major zones ere distinguished. The limit of the Laminaria zone was at a depth of about 10m; its extension was restricted rather by the absence of rock than by the attenuation of light due to depth. The rock gave way to small boulders and a mixture of silty mud and coarse gravel, characterised by large species such as Axinellid sponges, Polymastia and Asterias. In a depth of about 13.5m, this zone merged into a coarse shelly sand and mud mixture with occasional rock outcrops; here sand waves indicated bottom mobility. Flustra was present on the rock as well as Axinellid sponges, while Cerianthus, Corymorpha and Cancer were prominent on the mud. At about 17m depth (point z) stones and boulders began to appear with some mud and shell debris. The stones were characterised by the presence of Alcyonidium gelatinosum and Bugula spp. together with various hydroids such as Nemertesia spp..

The upper and mid shore pools were deep but those lower down were large and shallow. The lower shoe dropped steeply into the sea. The major cover was Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella with occasional fucoids.

column2: This was a bflatter area of bedrock. Dense red algae was present to a depth of 14m. A variety of sponges, hydroids and bryozoans were present.
column3: This was a flatter area of bedrock. Dense red algae was present to a depth of 14m. A variety of sponges, hydroids and bryozoans were present.

column2: This site appears to delimit the seawards extent of the Laminaria. Here the low-lying rocky reefs were interspersed among coarse, shelly sand at a depth of about 13m. Laminaria plants were sparse on the rocky outcrops but there were some red and smaller brown algae together with a fairly sparse `undergrowth fauna' of sponges such as Polymastia, bryozoans such as Bugula spp. and hydroids of which Nemertesia antennina was the most common. Abrasion by the mobile sand might restrict both flora and fauna in such an exposed area. The sand was rippled into small waves about 10cm in height and the only obvious infaunal species was the holothurian Neopentadactyla mixta which was capable of burroing up to 50cm in the sand.

column2: The bottom consisted of shelly mud and stones and prominent species included Crisiidae, Alcyonidium gelatinosum and Epizaonthus couchii.
column3: The bottom consisted of shelly mud and stones and prominent species included Crisiidae, Alcyonidium gelatinosum and Epizoanthus couchii.

column2: A flat rocky area facing south-easterly with boulders and some silty mud and sand, mainly covered by Ascophyllum, some Fucus vesiculosus and F. serratus. Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthaliaat lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, with other annelids.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshoroe. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay. Pelvetia, Ascophyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whilst lower own there were many overhangs and crevices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered with sand and silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders, together with Platynereis coccinea.

column2: This long narrow inlet had a northerly aspect with steep rocky sides widening out to a platform lower down. Pelvetia occurred at the top of the shore and the sides of the gulley bore a Chthamalus/Balanus/Patella cover. Balanus, Patella and Actinia extended well up the gulley walls; the floor was covered by Fucus, Balanus and Patella. Lower down there were shallow `ckoralline' pools and some deep cracks and overhangs. The upper part of the gulley was affected by freshwater runoff.

column2: This was a further extension of the gently sloping bedrock and boulder slope, with some kelp plants extending down to 12m. This was one o the few sites where Echinus esculentus was occasionally seen grazing in the kelp.

column2: This was a steep,jagged bedrock slope below the lower limits of red macrophytes, although Rhodymenia pseudopalmata was present.

column2: This shore, which had a south south-easterly aspect, had as its dominant (80-90% cover) Balanus/Patella with Chthamalus at the top. A shallow shelf occurred in the lower littoral with many gullies and coralline pools. Above this, the shore was steeper and backed by high cliffs. Laminaria and Alaria fringed the lower shore while Pelvetia occurred higher up. Between here and Maen Du there was a narrow steep-sided inlet, about 20-30m long with a boulder bottom; Chthamalus extends right to the top of this inlte.

In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, with other annelids.

column2: This long narrow inlet had a northerly aspect with steep rocky sides widening out to a platform lower down. Pelvetia occurred at the top of the shore and the sides of the gulley bore a Chthamalus/Balanus/Patella cover. Balanus, Patella and Actinia extended well up the gulley walls; the floor was covered by Fucus, Balanus and Patella. Lower down there were shallow `coralline' pools and some deep cracks and overhangs. Theb upper part of the gulley was affected by freshwater runoff.

column2: This was a steep, jagged bedrock slope below the lwoer limits of red macrophytes, although Rhodymenia pseudopalmata was present.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On tihs the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: This site appears to delimit the seawards extent of the Laminaria. Here the low-lying rocky reefs were interspersed among coarse, shelly sand at a depth of about 13m. Laminaria plants were sparse on the rocky outcrops but there were some red and smaller brown algae together with a fairly sparse `undergrowth fauna' of sponges such as Polymastia, bryozoans such as Bugula spp. and hydroids of which Nemertesia antennina was the most common. Abrasion by the mobile sand might restrict both flora and fauna in such an exposed area. The sand was rippled into small waves about 10cm in height and the only obvious infaunal species was the holothurian Neopentadactyla mixta which aws capable of burrowing up to 50cm in the sand.

column2: The slope continued gently dwn to a depth of about 20m, the boulders becoming smaller and less frequent, overlying stoney/shelly mud. Some of the brown algae such as Dictyota extended down the boulder slope and some red algae were still present at 18m. Hymeniacedon, Sagartia and Actinothoe were abundant on the boulder slope as well as Nemertesia spp..

column2: The whole surface must be highly mobile and is thown up into a series of large sand waves over 1m in height and running at right angles to the shore. The mobility and lack of hard substrates restricted the fauna to a few species of fish such as Plaice and Brill. Some hermit crabs, Pagurus bernhardus were also present. The landward edge of the bank slopes downwards at an angle of about 30 degrees to a depth of about 25m where boulders and rock outcrops begin to occur. Some tubes of the polychaete Lanice conchilega were present on the slope. Prominent species on the boulders were Nemertesia ramosa, Bugula turbinata, Tealia felina and Flustra foliacea. The latter two species have been recorded in areas of the Menai Strait which are similarly subject to gravel abrasion.
column3: The whole surface must be highly mobile and is thrown up into a series of large sand waves over 1m in height and running at right angles to the shore. The mobility and lack of hard substrates restricted the fauna to a few species of fish such as Plaice and Brill. Some hermit crabs, Pagurus bernhardus were also present. The landward edge of the bank slopes downwards at an angle of about 30 degrees to a depth of about 25m where boulders and rock outcrops begin to occur. Some tubes of the polychaete Lanice conchilega were present on the slope. Prominent species on the boulders were Nemertesia ramosa, Bugula turbinata, Tealia felina and Flustra foliacea. The latter two species have been recorded in areas of the Menai Strait which are similarly subject to gravel abrasion.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the idshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: A flat rocky area facing south-easterly with boulders and some silty mud and sand, mainly covered by Ascophyllum, some Fucus vesiculosus and F. serratus. Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthalia at lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often containewd shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, with other annelids.

column2: The fauna was found to be very rich and varied, the top of the pinnacle being covered in a dense growth of the red tunicate Dendrodoa grossularia as well as many colourful colonies of the jewel anemone Cotynactis viridis. Hydroids and sponges grew in profusion on the sides of the rock. The great depth of the water limited diving time to a few minutes but most of the prominent species are litsed.

column2: The shore faced WSW, the upper part was steep but lower down it flattened into an area of pools with encrusting coralline cover on the bottoms. The major cover was Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella with occaisonal fucoids.

column2: The Laminariaf orest extended out from the shore for a distance of approximately 50m where it ended at a depth of about 7m on a steep bedrock and large boulder slope.

column2: At a depth of about 18.5m the slope gave way to a muddy gravel plain where some hydrois such as Nemertesia ramosa and bryozoans such as Alcyonidium and Bugula were found attached to small stones.
column3: At a depth of about 18.5m the slope gave way to a muddy gravel plain where some hydroids such as Nemertesia ramosa and bryozoans such as Alcyonidium and Bugula were found attached to small stones.

column2: This site appears to delimit the seawards extent of the Laminaria. Here the low-lying rocky reefs were interspersed among coarse, shelly sand at a depth of about 13m. Laminaria plants were sparse on the rocky outcrops but there were some red and smaller brown algae together with a fairly sparse `undergrowth fauna' of sponges such as Polymastia, bryozoans such as Bugula spp. and hydroids of which Nemertesia antennina was the most common. Abrasion by the mobile sand might restrict both flora and fauna in such an exposed area. The sand was rippled into small waves about 10cm in height and the only obvious infaunal species was the holothurian Neopentadactyla mixta which was capable of burrowing up to 0cm in the sand.

column2: This long narrow inlet had a northerly aspect with steep rocky sides widening out to a platform lower down. Pelvetia occurred at the top of the shore and the sides of the gulley bore a Chthamalus/Balanus/Patella cover. Balanus, Patella and Actinia extended well up the gulley walls; the floor was coverd by Fucus, Balanus and Patella. Lower down there were shallow `coralline' pools and some deep cracks and overhangs. The upper part of the gulley was affected by freshwater runoff.

column2: Gentle bedrock slope was replaced by boulders at around 11m. column2: At this site, at the Southern tip of the Island, the shore was composed of broken, rocky slopes with a south-west aspect. These rocks were mainly Balanus covered, with Chthamalus belt at the top of the shore. Some Pelveti awas present. At one point a limestone band 2m wide extended right up the shore; it's surface was pitted with depressions occupied by Patella and was covered by encrusting coralline algae and Corallina to the top of the eulittoral. Some Chondrus was also present amongst high-shore Pelvetia, an indication of the great exposure to waves and the water retaining property of the eroded limestone surface. The most exposed parts of this shore were not visited.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay.r Pelvetia, Ascophyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whilst lower down there were many overhangs and crevices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered with sand and silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders, together with Platynereis coccinea.

column2: A steep grassy slope made access to the rocky shore difficult. The upper and mid shore was steep but qthe slope eased lower down to form a platform with shallow coralline pools. Apart from red algae on the lower shore and a Chthamalus belt at the top the shore was dominated by Balanus (70%-80% cover) and Patella.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella comumnity, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: This site appears to delimit the seawards extent of the Laminaria. Here the low-lying rocky reefs were interspersed among coarse, shelly sand at a depth of about 13m. Laminaria plants were sparse on the rocky outcrops but there were some red and smaller brown algae together with a fairly sparse `undergrowth fauna' of sponges such as Polymastia, bryoqzoans such as Bugula spp. and hydroids of which Nemertesia antennina was the most common. Abrasion by the mobile sand might restrict both flora and fauna in such an exposed area. The sand was rippled into small waves about 10cm in height and the only obvious infaunal species was the holothurian Neopentadactyla mixta which was capable of burrowing up to 50cm in the sand.

column2: The Laminaria forest extended out from the shore for a distance of apprximately 50m where it ended at a depth of about 7m on a steep bedrock and large boulder slope.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay. Pelvetia, Ascophyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whilst lower down there were many overahngs and crevices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered with sand and silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders, together with Platynereis coccinea.

The majora cover was Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella with occasional fucoids.

column2: The whole slope was comparatively steep, resulting in a narrow Laminaria band. A variety of hydroids and sponges were present. column2: This shore, which had a south south-easterly aspect, had as its domniant (80-90% cover) Balanus/Patella with Chthamalus at the top. A shallow shelf occurred in the lower littoral with many gullies and coralline pools. Above this, the shore was steeper and backed by high cliffs. Laminaria and Alaria fringed the lower shore while Pelvetia occurred higher up. Between here and Maen Du there was a narrow steep-sided inlet, about 20-30m long with a boulder bottom; Chthamalus extends right to the top of this inlet.

column2: A rocky shore of east south-easterly aspect with steep sides facing the sea but with extensive flat areas behind with coralline pools and Pelvetia-covered rocks. column2: These steep, barnacle covered shores faced NNE, and were backed by cliffs; small rock platforms occurred lower down the shore. Fucoids were rare, the main cover consisting fo Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella around a few coralline pools.

column2: The slope continued gently down to a depth of about 20m, the boulders becoming smaller and less frequent, overlying stoney/shelly mud. Some of the brown algae such as Dictyota extended down the boulder slope and some red algae were still rpesent at 18m. Hymeniacedon, Sagartia and Actinothoe were abundant on the boulder slope as well as Nemertesia spp..

column2: A flat rocky area facing south-easterly with boulders and some silty mud and sand, mainly covered by Ascophyllum,s ome Fucus vesiculosus and F. serratus. Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthalia at lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, with other annelids.

column2: Gentle bedrock slope was replaced by boulders at around 11m. column2: This long narrow inlet had a northerly aspect with steep rocky sides widening out to a platform lower down. Pelvetia occurred at the top of the shore and the sides of the gulley bore a Chthamalus/Balanus/Patella cover. Balanus, Patella and Actinia extended well up tphe gulley walls; the floor was covered by Fucus, Balanus and Patella. Lower down there were shallow `coralline' pools and some deep cracks and overhangs. The upper part of the gulley was affected by freshwater runoff.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay. Pelvetia, Ascophyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whiklst lower down there were many overhangs and crevices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered with sand and silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders, together with Platynereis coccinea.

A Barnacle/Patella cover was dominant, interspersed with occasional fucoids.

column2: This shore, which had a south south-easterly aspect, had as its dominant (80-90% cover) Balanus/Patella with Chthamalus at the top. A shallow shelf occurred in the lower littoral with many gullies and coralline pools. Above this, the shore was steeper and backed by high cliffs. Laminaria and Alaria fringed the lowershore while Pelvetia occurred higher up. Between here and Maen Du there was a narrow steep-sided inlet, about 20-30m long with a boulder bottom; Chthamalus extends right to the top of this inlet.

column2: The shore, which faced ENE, was backed by rocky cliffs and a steep grass slope. column2: This was a steep, jagged bedrock slope below the locwer limits of red macrophytes, although Rhodymenia pseudopalmata was present.

column2: At this site, at the Southern tip of the Island, the shore was composed of broken, rocky slopes with a south-west aspect. These rocks were mainly Balanus covered, with Chthamalus belt at the top of the shore. Some Pelvetia was present. At one point a limestone band 2m wide extended right up the shore; it's surface was pitted with depressions occupied by Patella and was covered by encrusting coralline algae and Corallina to the top of the eulittoral. Some Chondrus was also present amongst high-shore Pelvetia, an indication of the great exposure to waves and the water retaining property of the eroded limestone sfurface. The most exposed parts of this shore were not visited.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was bundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: Kelp forest dominated an area of steep bedrock. Algae comlpetely dominated this area apart from Nemertesia antennina and Pachymatisma johnstoni on vertical and overhanging surfaces. One Echinus esculentus was observed.

column2: The Laminaria forest extended down a bedrock slojpe to a depth of about 11m although some Laminaria plants occurred down to about 14m. Occasional large boulders were found within the Laminaria forest which sheltered many sponge and hydroid species as well as Corynactis viridis and the tunicate Clavelina lepadiformis. Red algae extended down to about 14m; below this stones and boulders intermixed with silt and shell material were found. The bottom levelled off at about 16m and was mostly of shelly mud. Bryozoans such as Cellaria and Crisia as well as Axinellid sponges were common in the deeper water and Epizoanthus couchii was present even on horizontal silt covered surfaces. Of all the diving sites this one must be considered the most sheltered from both currents and wave-action.

column2: This site seems characteristic of the middle west coast. The average depth was 16m and this was the limit of the occurrence of Laminaria plants. The bottom was broken up into a series of large outcrops and gullies covered with any sponges such as Halichondria, Pachymatisma and Axinella sp.. Hydroids like Nemertesia and Aglaophenia were also plentiful as well as bryozoans such as Crisia, Flustra and Bugula. Alcyonium digitatum was common and Sagartia elegans frequent. Echinus esculentus, Homarus and Palinurus were also present. It is this area of the Bardsey coast which seems particularly attractive to visiting groups of divers.

column2: This long narrow inlet had a northerly aspect with steep rocky sides widening out to a platform lower down. Pelvetia occurred at the top of the shore and the sides of the gulley bore a Chthamalus/Balanus/Patella cover. Balanus, Patella and Actinia extended well up the gulley walls; the floor was covered byr Fucus, Balanus and Patella. Lower down there were shallow `coralline' pools and some deep cracks and overhangs. The upper part of the gulley was affected by freshwater runoff.

column2: A flat rocky area facing south-easterly with boulders and some silty mud and sand, mainly covered by Ascophyllum, some Fucus vesiculosus and F. serratus. Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthalia at lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover zwas low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, with other annelids.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay. Pelvetia, Ascophyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whilst lower down there were many overhangs and crevices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered wvith sand and silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders, together with Platynereis coccinea.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and sowme Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: This was an area of steep bedrock, outcrops and gullies. Some prominent species present in the kelp forest included Corynactis viridis, Halichondria panicea and Aglaohpenia pluma.

column2: Ths was a very gently sloping area of shelly mud and stones. Pentapora foliacea and tufts of Aglaophenia tubulifera were occasionally noted. All the rocks below the algal zone possessed a dense `undergrowth fauna' of hydroids and still more numerous bryozoans such as Bugula spp. Off Pen Cristin and to the south west are several large rocky reefs rising up to 12m from the bottom. These bore a rich variety of sponges (Axinellids), anemones such as Epizoanthus couchi and Corynactis viridis as well as lobsters and crawfish.

column2: This was a flatter area of bedrock. Dense red algae wasp resent to a depth of 14m. A variety of sponges, hydroids and bryozoans were present.
column3: This was a flatter area of bedrock. Dense red algae was present to a depth of 14m. A variety of sponges, hydroids and bryozoans were present.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes conntaining anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: Gentle bedrock slope was replaced by boulders at around 11m. There was a striking lack rof fauna on flat surfaces in the algal zone which was almost completely dominated by the algae except on features such as vertical surfaces and overhangs where prominent species including Nemertesia antennina and Pachymatisma johnstoni occurred.
column3: Gentle bedrock slope was replaced by boulders at around 11m. There was a striking lack of fauna on flat surfaces in the algal zone which was almost completely dominated by the algae except on features such as vertical surfaces and overhangs where prominent species including Nemertesia antennina and Pachymatisma johnstoni occurred.

column2: The Laminaria forest extended down a bedrock slope to a depth of about 11m although some Laminaria plants occurred down to about 14m. Occasional large boulders were found within the Laminaria forest which sheltered many sponge and hydroid species as well as Corynactis viridis and the tunicate Clavelina lepadiformis. Red algae extended down to about 14m; below this stones and boulders intermixed with silt and shell material were found. The bottom levelled off at about 16m and was mostly of shelly mud. Bryozoans such as Cellaria and Crisia as well as Axinellid sponges were common in the deeper water and Epizoanthus couchii was present even on horizontal silt covered surfaces. Of all the diving sites this one must be considered the most sheltered from both currents and wavea-ction.

column2: A flat rocky area facing south-easterly with boulders and some silty mud and sand, mainly covered by Ascophyllum, some Fucus vesiculosus and F. serratus. Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthalia at lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, wih other annelids.

column2: This was an area of steeply sitepping bedrock, with relatively low species diversity.

column2: Kelp forest dominated an area of steep bedrock. Algae completely dominated this area apart from Nemertesia antennina and Pachymatisma johnstoni on vertical and overhanging surfaces. On Echinus esculentus was observed.
column3: Kelp forest dominated an area of steep bedrock. Algae completely dominated this area apart from Nemertesia antennina and Pachymatisma johnstoni on vertical and overhanging surfaces. One Echinus esculentus was observed.

column2: At this site, at the Southern tip of the Island, hte shore was composed of broken, rocky slopes with a south-west aspect. These rocks were mainly Balanus covered, with Chthamalus belt at the top of the shore. Some Pelvetia was present. At one point a limestone band 2m wide extended right up the shore; it's surface was pitted with depressions occupied by Patella and was covered by encrusting coralline algae and Corallina to the top of the eulittoral. Some Chondrus was also present amongst high-shore Pelvetia, an indication of the great exposure to waves and the water retaining property of the eroded limestone surface. The most exposed parts of this shore were not visited.

column2: The shore faced WSW, the upper part was steep but lower down it flattened into an area of pools with encrusting coralline cover on the bottoms. The upper and mid shore pools were deep but those lower down were large and shallow. The lower shore dropped steepy into the sea. The major cover was Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella with occasional fucoids.

column2: The fauna was found to be very rich and varied, the top of the pinnacle being covered in a dense growth of the red tunicate Dendrodoa grossularia as well as many colourful colonies of the jewel anemone Cotynactis viridis. Hydroids and sponges grew in profusion on the sides of the rock. The greqat depth of the water limited diving time to a few minutes but most of the prominent species are listed.

column2: A flat rocky area facing south-easterly with boulders and some silty mud and sand, mainly covered by Ascophyllum, some Fucus vesiculosus and F. serratus. Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthalia at lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms;v at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, with other annelids.

column2: This shore was a boulder and rock platform facing West and covered in Ascophyllum and Fucus serratus with some Fucus vesiculosus. Barnacle cover was low and large limpets occurred under the algae. Many ridges, gullies, crevices and overhangs wree present.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of te boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: This was a very gently sloping area of shelly mud and stones. Pentapora foliacea and tufts of Aglaophenia tubulifera were occasionally noted. All the rocks below the algal zone possessed a dense `undergrowth fauna' of hydroids and still more numerous bryozoans such as Bugula spp. Off Pen Cristin and to the south west are several large rocky reefs rising up to 12m from the bottom. These bore a rich variety of sponges (Axinellids), anemones such as Epizoanthus couchi and Corynactis viridis as well as lobsters ad crawfish.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay. Pelvetia, Ascohpyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whilst lower down there were many overhangs and crevices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered with sand and silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders, together with Platynereis coccinea.

column2: A steep grassy slope made access to the rocky shore difficult. The upper and mid shore was steep but the slope eased lower down to form a platfor with shallow coralline pools. Beyond this there was a steep drop into the sublittoral, fringed with Alaria and Laminaria. Apart from red algae on the lower shore and a Chthamalus belt at the top the shore was dominated by Balanus (70%-80% cover) and Patella.

column2: This was the only site which consisted mostly of soft bottom material occasionally interrupted by a small rock outcrop. Three major zones were distinguished. The limit of the Laminaria zone was at a depth of about 10;m its extension was restricted rather by the absence of rock than by the attenuation of light due to depth. The rock gave way to small boulders and a mixture of silty mud and coarse gravel, characterised by large species such as Axinellid sponges, Polymastia and Asterias. In a depth of about 13.5m, this zone merged into a coarse shelly sand and mud mixture with occasional rock outcrops; here sand waves indicated bottom mobility. Flustra was present on the rock as well as Axinellid sponges, while Cerianthus, Corymorpha and Cancer were prominent on the mud. At about 17m depth (point z) stones and boulders began to appear with some mud and shell debris. The stones were characterised by the presence of Alcyonidium gelatinosum and Bugula spp. together with various hydroids such as Nemertesia spp..

The major cover was Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella with occasional fucoids.

column2: This flat rock and boulder shore had a Westerly aspcet; the top being bare and slimy. There were many large and small `coralline' pools. Balanus cover up to 50% occurred in places together with abundant Patella and some fucoids.

column2: The shore faced WSW, the upper part was steep but lower down it flattened into an area of pools with encrusting coralline cover on the bottoms. The major cover was Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella with occasional fucoids.

column2: This site seems characteristic of the middle west coast. The average depth was 16m and this was teh limit of the occurrence of Laminaria plants. The bottom was broken up into a series of large outcrops and gullies covered with many sponges such as Halichondria, Pachymatisma and Axinella sp.. Hydroids like Nemertesia and Aglaophenia were also plentiful as well as bryozoans such as Crisia, Flustra and Bugula. Alcyonium digitatum was common and Sagartia elegans frequent. Echinus esculentus, Homarus and Palinurus were also present. It is this area of the Bardsey coast which seems particularly attractive to visiting groups of divers.

column2: At this site, at the Southern tip of the Island, the shore was composed of broken, rocky slopes with a south-west aspect. These rocks were mainly Balanus covered, with Chthamalus belt at the top of the shore. Some Pelvetia was present. At one point a limestone band 2m wide extended right up the shore; it's surface was pitted with depressions occupied by Patella and was covered by encrusting coralline algae and Corallina to the top of the eulittoral. Some Chondrus was also present amongst high-shore Pelrvetia, an indication of the great exposure to waves and the water retaining property of the eroded limestone surface. The most exposed parts of this shore were not visited.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay. Pelvetia, Ascophyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whilst lower down there were many overhangs and creices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered with sand and silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders, together with Platynereis coccinea.

column2: This was a very gently sloping area of shelly mud and stoens. Pentapora foliacea and tufts of Aglaophenia tubulifera were occasionally noted. All the rocks below the algal zone possessed a dense `undergrowth fauna' of hydroids and still more numerous bryozoans such as Bugula spp. Off Pen Cristin and to the south west are several large rocky reefs rising up to 12m from the bottom. These bore a rich variety of sponges (Axinellids), anemones such as Epizoanthus couchi and Corynactis viridis as well as lobsters and crawfish.

Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthalia at lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was fairly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, with other annelids.

column2: A steep grassy slope made access to the rocky shore difficult. The upper and mid shore was steep but the slope eased lower down to form a platform with shallow coralline pools. Beyond this there was a steep drop into the sublittoral, fringed with Alaria and Laminraia. Apart from red algae on the lower shore and a Chthamalus belt at the top the shore was dominated by Balanus (70%-80% cover) and Patella.

column2: Gentle bedrock slope was replaced by boulders at around 11m. There was a striking lack of fauna on flat surfaces in the algal zone which wa salmost completely dominated by the algae except on features such as vertical surfaces and overhangs where prominent species including Nemertesia antennina and Pachymatisma johnstoni occurred.

column2: The bottom consisted of shelly mud and stones and prominent species included Crisidae, Alcyonidium gelatinosum and Epizoanthus couchii.
column3: The bottom consisted of shelly mud and stones and prominent species included Crisiidae, Alcyonidium gelatinosum and Epizoanthus couchii.

column2: A series of quite steep rises and falls resulted in a narrow algal zone - the Laminaria forest extended only a few metres from the shore. In the deeper water between about 12.5m and 17.5m there was a carpet of red algae but at a depth of about 20m the algae ceased to grow. Below this there was a slope of large boulders with pockets of stony graevl. Alcyonium digitatum and Tubularia indivisa were common on the boulders. The larger boulders gave way to smaller ones on the bottom at a depth of about 30m.

column2: Backed y low cliffs of boulder clay, this medium-sloping rock flattened out at the lower levels; its aspect was SW. Pelvetia was present above; encrusting corallines covered most of the lower shore. There were many shallow coralline pools. A Barnacle/Patella cover was dominant, interspersed with occasional fucoids.
column3: Backed by low cliffs of boulder clay, this medium-sloping rock flattened out at the lower levels; its aspect was SW. column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. ower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: This was a flat rock and boulder shore with a Westerly aspect. The top of the shore was bare and slimy. Many large and small `coralline' dominated pools were present. Balanus balanoides had up to 50% coevr in some places, with abundant Patella vulgata and some fucoids.
column3: This was a flat rock and boulder shore with a Westerly aspect. The top of the shore was bare and slimy. Many large and small `coralline' dominated pools were present. Balanus balanoides had up to 50% cover in some places, with abundant Patella vulgata and some fucoids.

column2: Steep rock at the top of the shore sheled off to a platform fringed by Laminaria. The dominant cover was Chthamalus spp. and Balanus balanoides, with occasional fucoids. Some shallow coralline pools were present.
column3: Steep rock at the top of the shore shelved off to a platform fringed by Laminaria. The dominant cover was Chthamalus spp. and Balanus balanoides, with occasional fucoids. Some shallow coralline pools were present.

column2: This site appears to delimit the seawards extent of the Laminaria. Here the low-lying rocky reefs were interspersed among coarse, shelly sand at a depth of about 13m. Laminaria plants were sparse on the rocky outcrops but there were some red and smaller brown algae together with a fairly sparse `undergrowth fauna' of sponges such as Polymastia, bryozoans such as Bugula spp. and hydroids of which Nemertesia antennina was the most common. Abrasion by the mobile sand might restrict both flora and fauna in such an exposed area. The sand was rippled into small waves about 10cm in height and the only obvious infaunal species cwas the holothurian Neopentadactyla mixta which was capable of burrowing up to 50cm in the sand.

column2: This is a small rocky outcrop frequented by seals and lying about 500m west of Porth Solfach. On the landward side the water was very shallow (about 4m deep) and the Laminaria forest, broken up by patches of sand, extended out to the reef from the main island. Close to the reef on the seaward side the depth was about 10m and although L. hyperborea was present it was not as dense as on the east cpoast. The bedrock was broken up into a series of large gullies, some with small stones and sediment on the bottom. Most of the flat rock surfaces were covered with algae but on vertical faces, and under overhangs, the sponges, hydroids and bryozoans were again common. Nemertesia antennina was rare at this site and no N. ramosa were seen at all. The starfish Marthasterias glacialis was later recorded (A.R. Andrews, personal communication) from this site but it was never seen elsewhere.

column2: This was a very gently sloping area of shelly mud and stones. Pentapora foliacea and tufts of Aglaopenia tubulifera were occasionally noted. column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audeouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.

column2: This beach faced WNW, and consisted of a rocky shelf with boulders, sand and shingle at the top. Large areas of drift weed were found to accumulate in the bay. Pelvetia, Ascophyllum, Fucus vesiculosus and Fucus serratus were very common and the barnacle cover was about 30%. Many sandy-bottomed pools occurred in midshore whilst lower down there were many overhangs and crevices. On the lower shore the rocks were covered with sand nad silt; some Codium is found at this level. Near low water more gravel occurs. Arenicoloides ecaudata was found under boulders, together with Platynereis coccinea.

column2: A series of quite steep rises and falls resulted in a narrow algal zone - the Laminaria forest extended only a few metres from the shore. In the deeper water between about 12.5m and 17.5m there was a carpet of red algae but at a depth of about 20m the algae ceased to grzow. Below this there was a slope of large boulders with pockets of stony gravel. Alcyonium digitatum and Tubularia indivisa were common on the boulders. The larger boulders gave way to smaller ones on the bottom at a depth of about 30m.

column2: The whole surface must be highly mobile and is thrown up into a series of large sand waves over 1m in height ad running at right angles to the shore. The mobility and lack of hard substrates restricted the fauna to a few species of fish such as Plaice and Brill. Some hermit crabs, Pagurus bernhardus were also present. The landward edge of the bank slopes downwards at an angle of about 30 degrees to a depth of about 25m where boulders and rock outcrops begin to occur. Some tubes of the polychaete Lanice conchilega were present on the slope. Prominent species on the boulders were Nemertesia ramosa, Bugula turbinata, Tealia felina and Flustra foliacea. The latter two species have been recorded in areas of the Menai Strait which are similarly subject to gravel abrasion.

column2: Gentle bedrock slope was replaced by boulders at around 11m. There was a striking lack of fauna on flat surfaces in the algal zone which wasw almost completely dominated by the algae except on features such as vertical surfaces and overhangs where prominent species including Nemertesia antennina and Pachymatisma johnstoni occurred.

column2: A flat rocky area facing south-easterly with boulders and some silty mud and sand, mainly covered by Ascophyllum, some Fucus vesiculosus and F. serratus. Pelvetia was found on the upper shore and much Himanthalia at lower levels and in pools. In the upper littoral, there were shallow pools with rock bottoms; at lower levels, the pools often contained shingle. On the southern side of the bay there were many gullies, overhangs and crevices containing a rich anemone and sponge community. Patella was faierly common but Balanus cover was low over the whole bay. The fauna under the boulders was dominated by the tail-less lugworm Arenicola ecaudata, with other annelids.

column2: This flat rock and boulder shore had  Westerly aspect; the top being bare and slimy. There were many large and small `coralline' pools. Balanus cover up to 50% occurred in places together with abundant Patella and some fucoids.
column3: This flat rock and boulder shore had a Westerly aspect; the top being bare and slimy. There were many large and small `coralline' pools. Balanus cover up to 50% occurred in places together with abundant Patella and some fucoids.

column2: At this site, at the Southern tip of the Island, the shore was composed of broken, rocky slopes with a south-west aspect. These rocks were mainly Balanus covered, with Chthamalus belt at the top of the shore. Some Pelvetia was present. At one point a limestone band 2m wide extended right up the shore; it's surface was pitted with depressions occupied by Patella and was covered by encrusting coralline algae and Corallina to the top of the eulittoral. Some Chondrus was also present amongst high-shore Pelvetia, an indication of the great exposure to waves and the water retaining properyt of the eroded limestone surface. The most exposed parts of this shore were not visited.

column2: These steep, barnacle covered shores faced NNE, and were backed by cliffs; small rock platforms occurred lower down the shore. Fucoids were rare, the main coverz consisting of Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella around a few coralline pools.

column2: The shore faced WSW, the upper part was steep but lower down it flattened into an area of pools with encrusting coralline cover on the bottoms. The upper and mid shore pools were dee pbut those lower down were large and shallow. The lower shore dropped steeply into the sea. The major cover was Balanus, Chthamalus and Patella with occasional fucoids.

column2: This was a flatter area of bedrock. Dense red algae was presesnt to a depth of 14m. A variety of sponges, hydroids and bryozoans were present.

column2: hTis was an area of steep bedrock, outcrops and gullies. Kelp park species included Polyneura gmelinii, Scypha coronata and Gymnangium montagui.

column2: This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Ptolydora were found in the limestone.

column2: A series of quite steep rises and falls resulted in a narrow algal zone - the Laminaria forest extended only a fw metres from the shore. In the deeper water between about 12.5m and 17.5m there was a carpet of red algae but at a depth of about 20m the algae ceased to grow. Below this there was a slope of large boulders with pockets of stony gravel. Alcyonium digitatum and Tubularia indivisa were common on the boulders. The larger boulders gave way to smaller ones on the bottom at a depth of about 30m.
